coredata.dc:title: Aggregation induced emission “Turn on” ultra-low detection of anti-inflammatory drug flufenamic acid in human urine samples by carbon dots derived from bamboo stem waste

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dot-based fluorescence sensors have attracted research interest for the selective determination of anti-inflammatory drugs in biological fluids and environments. The overdose and accumulation of anti-inflammatory drugs in tissues can cause chronic side effects including abdominal pain, and renal damage. Herein, we report a new fluorescent probe, bamboo stem waste-derived carbon dots (BS-CDs) for highly sensitive detection of Flufenamic acid (FA), a hazardous anti-inflammatory drug. The UV–vis absorption spectra of BS-CDs show a redshifted absorption peak at 283 nm upon the addition of FA suggesting strong binding interaction between BS-CDs and FA molecule. The BS-CDs showed a fluorescence enhancement (∼2-fold) detection for FA (400 μM) in the linear concentration range (0.40 → 0.65 μM) with a limit of detection (LoD; 17 nM) and binding constant (Ka = 1.33 × 10−3 M−1). The time-resolved fluorescence decay analysis showed that the average lifetime of BS-CDs has slightly changed (4.42 → 4.67 ns) by the interaction with FA through the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) process. The interference, pH, and effect of time results suggest that BS-CDs are highly selective probes for FA detection and do not show any interference involvement during FA detection. The confirmation of the structure and morphology changes of BS-CDs after interaction with FA was carried out by XRD, FESEM, HRTEM, FTIR, and Raman spectroscopy. The practicability of the BS-CDs probe was proved by the detection of FA in human urine samples with recovery of 103–109 %. This suggests that the proposed BS-CDs-based ‘turn-on’ sensor could be used to determine the FA in biological fluids.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of bs-cds (100 μM) in the presence of Uric Acid (UA), Hydroquinone (HQ), Glycine (Gy), Mefenamic Acid (MA), and Flufenamic Acid (FA) (2.5 μM), and (b) corresponding bar diagram (λex: 380 nm, λem: 435 nm, slit width 5 nm).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of bs-cds upon successive addition of fa (10 → 100 μL), (b) corresponding linear fit. [BS-CDs] = 100 μM; [FA] = 400 μM; (λex: 380 nm, λem: 435 nm, slit width 5 nm), and (c) schematic representation of BS-CDs + FA complex formation for the aggregation process.
(a) Time-resolved decay of bs-cds in the absence and presence of fa (5, 7, 10, and 15 μL) (λex: 450 nm), [BS-CDs] = 100 μM; [FA] = 400 μM.

head.ce:title: Aggregation induced emission “Turn on” ultra-low detection of anti-inflammatory drug flufenamic acid in human urine samples by carbon dots derived from bamboo stem waste

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dot-based fluorescence sensors have attracted research interest for the selective determination of anti-inflammatory drugs in biological fluids and environments. The overdose and accumulation of anti-inflammatory drugs in tissues can cause chronic side effects including abdominal pain, and renal damage. Herein, we report a new fluorescent probe, bamboo stem waste-derived carbon dots (BS-CDs) for highly sensitive detection of Flufenamic acid (FA), a hazardous anti-inflammatory drug. The UV–vis absorption spectra of BS-CDs show a redshifted absorption peak at 283 nm upon the addition of FA suggesting strong binding interaction between BS-CDs and FA molecule. The BS-CDs showed a fluorescence enhancement (∼2-fold) detection for FA (400 μM) in the linear concentration range (0.40 → 0.65 μM) with a limit of detection (LoD; 17 nM) and binding constant (Ka = 1.33 × 10−3 M−1). The time-resolved fluorescence decay analysis showed that the average lifetime of BS-CDs has slightly changed (4.42 → 4.67 ns) by the interaction with FA through the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) process. The interference, pH, and effect of time results suggest that BS-CDs are highly selective probes for FA detection and do not show any interference involvement during FA detection. The confirmation of the structure and morphology changes of BS-CDs after interaction with FA was carried out by XRD, FESEM, HRTEM, FTIR, and Raman spectroscopy. The practicability of the BS-CDs probe was proved by the detection of FA in human urine samples with recovery of 103–109 %. This suggests that the proposed BS-CDs-based ‘turn-on’ sensor could be used to determine the FA in biological fluids.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid growth of the population, the demand for essential drug production has also increased in the pharmaceutical industry [1]. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are widely used in the broader category of non-opioid analgesics, analgesics, and antipyretics. These anti-inflammatory drugs reduce inflammation, minimizing its direct effect on pain-nerve stimulation and inhibiting prostaglandin formation at the site of damaged tissue [2–4]. Furthermore, the NSAIDs as ligands, including mefenamic acid [5], diclofenac [6,7], diflunisal [8], flufenamic acid (FA) [9], tolmentin [10], ibuprofen and naproxen [10,11], indomethacin [12], and suprofen [12,13] have been structurally characterized. FA has been generally used for the treatment of inflammation associated with chronic pain, osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, postoperative conditions, and menstrual cramps. While it is effective in treating these conditions, it is known to cause gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding, especially when used to treat musculoskeletal and joint disorders [14]. It is mostly metabolized through hydroxylation and glucuronidation, with a small amount of the original drug being excreted [15]. Notably, approximately 50 % of FA dosage is eliminated through human urine, and the remaining 30–40 % of unreacted FA is absorbed by the gastrointestinal tract which can cause severe chronic side effects like abdominal pain and renal damage [16–18]. So it is essential to selectively determine the presence of FA concentration in humans and the environment.

Various techniques, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [19], liquid chromatography-mass spectroscopy (LC-MS) [20], capillary electrophoresis [21], spectrofluorimetric analysis [22], electrochemical sensing [23,24] and flow injection-solid phase spectroscopy with photochemically induced fluorescence have been developed to determine FA levels [25]. These conventional analytical techniques are expensive and not portable; however, recognition-based fluorescence sensing is preferred for its simplicity, high sensitivity, and ease of use [26–30]. Fluorescent sensors based on carbon nanostructures including activated carbon, carbon dots (CDs), and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have been explored to determine anti-inflammatory, bioimaging, drug delivery, and catalysis applications [31–35]. The Aggregation-induced emission (AIE)-based nano fluorescent sensors have strong luminescence emission, long fluorescence lifetime, high photostability, and inherent biocompatibility [36–40]. Recently, bamboo stem-derived carbon dots (BS-CDs) have gained prominence in research attraction due to their advantages such as sustainable production, abundance, and cost-effectiveness. These CDs exhibit biocompatible properties and possess a highly selective ability to detect analytes in the environment, pharmaceutical drugs, and clinical screening [41–45]. Therefore, it is essential to carefully design a structurally modifying material that could promote highly selective and sensitive detection of FA. However, scholarly literature has not extensively explored the use of CDs derived from bamboo stem waste material for the detection of FA present in the environment.

In this work, we report an ultra-low detection of FA by CDs derived from bamboo stem biomass waste. The structural, morphological, and optical properties change of BS-CDs upon interaction with FA is evaluated. Furthermore, to optimize the fluorescence detection of FA, fluorescence decay profile, interference studies, and the effect of pH and time were also investigated. The proposed detection method was successfully validated using human urine samples, which demonstrated a reasonable recovery rate. This study emphasizes several benefits, one of which is an affordable and ecologically friendly way to use healthcare technology.

Flufenamic acid (C14H10F3NO2; 98 %), Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH; 98 %), Sodium Sulphate (Na2SO4; 99.9 %), Potassium Permanganate (KMnO4; 99 %), Magnesium Nitrate (Mg(NO3)2; 99 %), Calcium Chloride (CaCl2; 99 %), L-Ascorbic Acid (C6H8O6; 99 %), Uric Acid (C5H4N4O3; 99 %), Glycine (NH2CH2COOH; 99 %), Hydrochloric acid (HCl; 99 %) Ethanol (CH3CH2OH; 99.9 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The Hydroquinone (C6H6O2; 99 %) and Mefenamic Acid (C15H15NO2; 98 %) were purchased from a local pharmacy. All the experiments were conducted using deionized water (DI).

X-ray diffraction analysis was carried out in an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany) using Cu-Kα radiation with a Ni filter (λ = 1.5418 Å). UV–visible absorption spectra were performed on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1900 SHIMADZU, Japan). The photoluminescence spectra analysis was measured on a fluorescence spectrometer (JASCO FP-8250) at KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology, Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, India. The structural morphology was analyzed by FESEM (ZEISS (SIGMA), BRUDER) at Coimbatore Institute of Technology in Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, India, and by HRTEM (200 KV model JEOJEM 2100) at Sathyabama University, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India. The lifetime of photoluminescence was recorded by an IBH time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) system, and the decay curve was untangled by IBH data station software V2.6 at Bharathiyar University in Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, India. FT-IR spectra analysis was conducted using IR Affinity-1S (MIRacle-10) at the Avinashilingam Institute for Home Science and Higher Education for Women in Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, India. Raman spectroscopy analysis was measured on Horiba LabRAM-HR Raman spectrophotometer with a 532 nm excitation laser source at SAIF-Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala, India.

CDs from BS synthesized as per the literature procedure [46]. Briefly, 0.5 g of bamboo stem powder was dispersed in 30 mL of water under continuous stirring. Then suspension was poured into the 100 mL hydrothermal reactor and kept in a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 24 h. Finally, the obtained solution was centrifuged and separated using a membrane filter (0.22 μm) and stored at 5 °C for further utilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of stock solution (BS-CDs and FA)
ce:section.ce:para: The standard stock solution of FA (1 mM) was prepared in 10 mL of ethanol. A 2 mL of FA (1 mM) solution was diluted to 5 mL for the final concentration of FA (400 μM) and then the solution was sonicated for 5 min. To further characterize, 0.5 mL of BS-CDs + FA was drop-cast onto a glass plate and dried.

The fluorescence titration experiment was executed by measuring the changes in fluorescence emission of BS-CDs (100 μM; λem: 435 nm; λex: 380 nm) upon the consecutive addition of an FA (400 μM, 10 → 100 µL) into BS-CDs solution (100 μM, 2 mL). This analysis was carried out at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm with a slit width of 5 nm. TCSPC technique was used to study the excited state fluorescence lifetime for BS-CDs (100 μM) before and after the addition of FA.

To investigate the effect of pH on the fluorescence properties of BS-CDs, concentrated 0.5 M HCl and 1 M NaOH aqueous solutions were used to adjust the pH of BS-CDs from 1 to 13. This was done to ensure that the solution was at the optimum pH level for the subsequent reactions. Once the pH was adjusted, the solution was incubated for 5 min to allow for any necessary chemical reactions to take place. The incubation time was crucial for the binding interaction between the BS-CDs and FA. Finally, the corresponding PL spectra were recorded to evaluate the stability.

Drug-free urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers (age 20–50 years) and kept in a room environment (25 ± 0.1 °C). The urine samples were then filtered three times using a membrane filter with a pore size of 0.22 µm. Aliquots of 1 mL of the urine samples were spiked with a known concentration of FA (5.3 μM). Then spiked urine samples were added to the BS-CDs solution. The PL spectra were measured after 10 min incubation time. Recovery analysis were determined from spiked and measured samples.

The fluorescent material, BS-CDs, was synthesized in a one-pot hydrothermal method and the structure, morphology, and defects were identified by the standard physiochemical techniques [46] (Figs. S1-S4). Scheme 1 represents the interaction of BS-CDs with FA. Upon successive addition of FA into the BS-CDs solution, the fluorescence emission intensity of BS-CDs was enhanced possibly due to the AIE process by the interaction of FA with BS-CDs (λex 380 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and fluorescence studies of BS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of BS-CDs, FA, and BS-CDs in the presence of FA. The absorbance spectra of BS-CD show two peaks at 223 and 280 nm that correspond to π → π* and n → π* electron transitions associated with aromatic groups, respectively [47,48]. The UV–vis absorption spectra of FA exhibit two peaks at 286 and 345 nm [49]. Upon addition of FA to the BS-CDs, an absorption peak at 283 nm was identified with bathochromic shift (∼3 nm) compared with BS-CDs and exhibited blue emission under UV light irradiation (Fig. 1a inset) [50]. These results confirmed that BS-CDs possess a high binding interaction with FA.

The emission spectra of BS-CDs at various excitation wavelengths (330–380 nm) are shown in Fig. 1b. The high emission peak of BS-CDs was observed at 435 nm under 380 nm excitation wavelength, and an additional peak at 419 nm appeared due to their surface functional groups consisting of oxygen and nitrogen atoms. The attachment of the COOH functional group on the surface of the BS-CDs shows high binding interaction with FA. Based on the excitation-dependent emission spectra analysis, the excitation wavelength of BS-CDs was fixed to 380 nm and used for further fluorescence studies [51–53].

The selectivity of BS-CDs sensor was investigated with various anti-inflammatory drugs such as FA, Uric Acid (UA), Hydroquinone (HQ), Glycine (Gy), and Mefenamic Acid (MA). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the BS-CDs' fluorescence emission intensity was enhanced by adding FA, while all other anti-inflammatory drugs cause a fluorescence quenching effect, which is negligible, indicating the high selectivity of BS-CDs towards FA [54]. Based on this phenomenon, it is confirmed that the BS-CDs show a significant selectivity towards FA among the other anti-inflammatory drugs tested (Fig. 2b).

To use BS-CDs as sensitive anti-inflammatory drug sensing material, a fluorescence titration was conducted by successive addition of FA (400 μ M) into the BS-CDs solutions. It was observed that the BS-CDs (100 μM) fluorescence emission intensity was increased when different concentrations of FA (10 → 100 μL) were added resulting in the generation of a ground-state complex, BS-CDs•FA, (Fig. 3 a) [55]. This fluorescence enhancement might be due to the AIE process. Furthermore, the presence of FA in the BS-CDs solution may induce the self-agglomeration of BS-CDs through its strong binding with BS-CDs. The BS-CDs showed ∼2-fold fluorescence enhancement FA sensing in the linear concentration (0.40 → 0.65 μM) with a limit of detection (LoD) of 17 nM (Fig. 3b) and a binding constant of Ka = 1.33 × 10−3 M−1 (Fig. S1) [56,57]. It is believed that complex formation and extra-intramolecular interactions (Fig. 3c) are also responsible for the AIE process, which subsequently promotes the fluorescence enhancement of BS-CDs [58]. As shown in Fig. 3c, it is observed that the amine group of CDs is responsible for binding interaction with FA [59–61]. The BS-CDs particle sizes were increased from 20 nm to around 25 nm after the aggregation process (Fig. 6 HRTEM images). Therefore, we strongly believe the AIE process is responsible for the fluorescence enhancement of BS-CDs upon detecting FA. This research represents a significant step towards harnessing the potential of BS-CDs for the detection of FA in the environment.

It is essential to study the interference of various analytes such as metal ions, amino acids, biological molecules, and anti-inflammatory drugs on the efficient binding of BS-CDs with FA (Fig. S2a and b). For this reason, the interference effect of metal ions (Na2+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+), biomolecules (glycine and ascorbic acid), and anti-inflammatory drugs (Hydroquinone, Mefenamic acid, and Uric acid) at the higher concentrations (100 mM) on BS-CDs + FA was examined [63]. The addition of FA into the BS-CDs exhibits an enhanced fluorescence intensity, whereas all other metal ions, biomolecules, and other anti-inflammatory drugs do not affect or change the emission intensity of BS-CDs + FA. However, there may be a slight fall in the fluorescence emission intensity of the BS-CDs due to the dilution effect. From this result, it is concluded that BS-CDs have strong binding with FA and the presence of other interference does not affect the binding of FA with BS-CDs.

To investigate the fast sensing performance of BS-CDs, the fluorescence intensity changes of BS-CDs under various time intervals were investigated before and after the addition of FA. The emission intensity of BS-CDs at 435 nm is rapidly enhanced and reaches its almost 100 % fluorescence intensity within 60 s by the insertion of FA and remains stable upon increasing time (up to 30 min). This result further indicates that BS-CDs and FA interact quickly to form a highly stable complex, which is responsible for fluorescence enhancement (Fig. S3a and b) [64].

The sensitivity of the BS-CDs could influence biological conditions [65]. To validate this hypothesis, the effect of pH on FA detection was determined in both acidic and basic conditions (Fig. S4). The fluorescence emission intensity of BS-CDs was decreased after the addition of FA at pH range 1–5 (λem 435 nm) (Fig. S4a). On the other hand, the emission intensity of BS-CDs increased with the addition of FA at neutral pH (pH 7) [66]. The surface functional groups viz. COOH and NH2 were highly deprotonated at low pH resulting in fluorescence quenching intensity. When pH was increased to 7, –COOH groups on the edges of BS-CDs became protonated (COO–) which turns to the high affinity of BS-CDs upon interaction with FA. However, the fluorescence quenching of BS-CDs was observed at pH 9, 11, and 13 in the presence of FA (Fig. S4b). These results suggest that basic pH prevents BS-CDs + FA the complex formation. Moreover, BS-CDS showed strong fluorescence intensity due to high protonation at pH 7. This pH effect may cause the selective binding of FA with BS-CDs. The obtained result suggests that neutral pH is suitable for the determination of FA (Fig. S4c) [67]. This indicates that the prepared BS-CDs are robust and stable even under high ionic strength. Based on these findings, it is concluded that BS-CDs can be utilized for clinical trials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of BS-CDs interaction with FA
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain structural, optical, and binding interaction details of BS-CDs with FA, various physicochemical characterizations were conducted. The as-prepared BS-CDs showed a large peak at 2θ 21°–29° attributed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. The two broad shoulder peaks within 2θ range from 40° to 50° and 57° to 68°, corresponding to the planes (100) and (103) (Fig. S5) [46,68]. The BS-CDs show a broad diffraction peak of 2θ 23°-28° attributed to amorphous carbon. Interestingly, the BS-CDs explore the changes in crystal structural properties after sensing FA. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the (002) plane displays a fluorine peak at 2θ 14.4° [69]. The other peaks appeared at 2θ 21.9°, 26.9°, 36.2°, and 45.9°, attributable to the (003), (022), (420), and (134) lattice planes well matched with reported literature [70]. According to these observed peaks, BS-CDs + FA is crystalline, which is caused by the carboxyl group of oxygen being able to accept a proton, which is manifested in the aldehyde interaction between the oxygen and the proton. As a result, the appearance of a new peak at 2θ 51.2° (710) matches fluorine atoms in FA further confirming the complex formation between the BS-CDs and FA [71]. The structural change of BS-CDs and the formation of a complex upon the interaction of FA are successfully confirmed by the XRD results.

FESEM analysis was carried out to observe the morphology changes of BS-CDs after sensing FA. Initially, BS-CDs exhibited spherical-shaped particles likely due to weak interparticle forces minimizing surface-free energy (Fig. S6) [46,72]. However, FESEM images of BS-CDs + FA show FA covered on the surface of BS-CDs and possesses a flake structure (Fig. S7a → d). These results indicate that the addition of FA to BS-CDs could change the morphology of BS-CDs.

Fig. 6 shows the HRTEM images of BS-CDs after the interaction of FA. The HRTEM images revealed that highly uniform spherical for bare BS-CDs (size 3.7 nm) was increased due to agglomeration (average particle size 20–25 nm) upon successive addition of FA into BS-CDs solution (Fig. 6a & inset). The Selected Area Diffraction Pattern (SAED) of BS-CDs shows the (002), (003), and (022) planes corresponding to the polycrystalline nature of BS-CDs retained even after interaction with FA (Fig. 6b & inset). Further, the HRTEM images exhibit FA is attached to the surface of BS-CDs due to high binding interaction between FA and BS-CDs (Fig. 6c and d). These HRTEM image results suggest that BS-CDs enhanced fluorescence emission is related to FA attachment which leads to AIE [73].

Raman spectroscopy studies were conducted to examine defects and structural characteristics of BS-CDs after sensing with FA (Fig. S9a). The Raman spectrum of BS-CDs + FA exhibits two bands at 1342 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1, corresponding to amorphous carbon and ordered graphitic carbon, respectively, which indicates the formation of the BS-CDs + FA complex [74]. The ratio of D (1342 cm−1) and G (1570 cm−1) integration intensity bands for pure BS-CDs (ID/IG) is 0.85 (Fig. S10a) [41]. However, upon complexation with FA, there is a slightly blue-shifted G band (∼2 cm−1) was observed for BS-CDs + FA. As a result, the number of phonons interacting with the incident photon increased, corresponding to the chemical bond length of molecules, resulting in an ID/IG ratio of 0.86 instead of less than one. This slightly increased ID/IG is because of increased structural defects in BS-CDs upon interaction with FA [75]. The additional peak at 1439 cm−1 is attributed to the CH2 vibration in BS-CDs + FA which is due to their electronic states or scattered photon energy levels [76]. The intensity of BS-CDs + FA is higher than that of bare BS-CDs (D band: 270 → 279 a.u and G band: 434 → 619 a.u) indicating a high number of defects and a high degree of graphitization in the BS-CDs + FA, which are favorable for accelerating electron and ion migration, thus improving fluorescence intensity [77].

The FTIR spectra of BS-CDs + FA (Fig. S9b) exhibit a strong peak at 3314, 2152, and 1641 cm−1 attributed to the OH stretching, CC bending, CC stretching vibrations, respectively, which are almost identical to bare BS-CDs (Fig. S10b) [78]. In addition, a new peak at 1529 cm−1 is observed corresponding to the C-N band. Further, the peaks for the presence of C(2)N and benzene rings, which are features of an FA molecule, were witnessed at 1329 and 578 cm−1 [79]. The presence of fluorine groups (CF) bending vibration bands was confirmed by the strong intense peaks at 1049 and 674 cm−1 [80]. This FTIR data supports the successfully formed complex, BS-CDs + FA, which is in good agreement with all the above-reported characterization data and the fluorescence enhancement in the sensing application.

To evaluate the stability of BS-CDs, we conducted a zeta potential analysis for BS-CDs solution. The CDs with a zeta potential value of <−10 mV are considered highly stable [81]. In the current system, an aqueous solution of BS-CDs have a lower zeta potential (−3.39 mV) which is due to the presence of carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl functional groups (Fig. S11). This zeta potential value indicate the synthesized BS-CDs are highly stable because of the electrostatic repulsion, and they do not require any surface agents for their long time stability [82].

In summary, bamboo stem biomass waste was converted into CDs using a hydrothermal process. BS-CDs with spherical morphologies were utilized for the sensitive determination of FA in urine samples. As a result, BS-CDs exhibit a 2-fold fluorescence enhancements when FA is added, resulting in high sensitivity for FA detection (LoD = 17 nM; Ka = 1.33 × 10−3 M−1). In the presence of protonated COO– groups on the surface of BS-CDs, FA formed complexe that enhanced their fluorescence at pH 7. Formation of a ground-state complex (BS-CDs + FA) through AIE process is the further evidence of BS-CDs and FA interaction. There was only a slight increase in the fluorescence average lifetime of BS-CDs after binding with FA (4.04 → 4.64 ns) indicates the strong interaction between BS-CDs and FA. The interference and time effect studies confirm that other interference does not affect the rapid binding of FA with BS-CDs. The FESEM and HRTEM microscope images of BS-CDs after FA sensing reveal the morphology changes from spherical to sheet like structure and confirmed the complexation which increases BS-CDs particle size (20–25 nm) due to the aggregation process. The XRD results indicate the crystallinity of BS-CDs + FA. Moreover, the obtained analytical results provide an inexpensive, convenient, and reliable method for detecting FA in human urine samples (Recovery: 100 %–109 %, RSD: <1.5 %). In our knowledge, this is the first work to utilize BS-CDs for sensitive fluorescence detection of FA at ultra-low concentrations, with an eco-friendly sustainability approach (Table S1) [23,24,83,85]. This report presents an environmental and biomedical fluorescent sensor for FA detection.
